candidate_sources.title: Bacterial canker of tomato | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial canker of tomato caused by Clavibacter michiganensis subsp. michiganensis. Symptoms initially appear on leaflet margins where they become yellow and necrotic and appear as though they have been burned on the edges. Stem splits forming long brown cankers; yellow sticky fluid may emerge from cut stem. Fruit may have small raised white spots with tan or brown centers (bird's eye spot). Management includes certified pathogen-free seed, sanitation of tools, and removal of infected plants.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Canker of Tomato | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial canker of tomato is caused by the seedborne bacterium Clavibacter michiganensis subsp. michiganensis. The pathogen systemically colonizes tomato xylem leading to unilateral leaflet wilt, marginal leaf necrosis, stem and petiole cankers, and plant death. Splash dispersal onto fruit exteriors causes bird's-eye lesions characterized as necrotic centers surrounded by white halos.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Canker of Tomato | Minnesota Department of Agriculture
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial canker of tomato (Clavibacter michiganensis subsp. michiganensis, Cmm) can cause significant losses in field and high tunnel tomatoes. The pathogen can be introduced in infected seeds or transplants, which often appear healthy at planting. Bacteria are sticky and easily move on hands and tools of workers and through splashing rain or irrigation. Symptoms include marginal leaf necrosis, wilt, and stem cankers.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Canker of Tomato: Revisiting a Global and Economically Damaging Seedborne Pathogen | Plant Disease
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial canker of tomato caused by Clavibacter michiganensis subsp. michiganensis (Cmm) is an economically damaging seedborne disease with worldwide distribution. The vascular pathogen invades and proliferates in xylem through natural openings or wounds, causing wilt and canker symptoms including unilateral leaflet wilt, marginal leaf necrosis, stem and petiole cankers, and bird's-eye fruit lesions.

candidate_sources.title: Clavibacter michiganensis (bacterial canker of tomato) | CABI Compendium
candidate_sources.snippet: CABI datasheet on Clavibacter michiganensis, the causal agent of bacterial canker of tomato. Covers identity, taxonomy, distribution, host range, symptoms (wilt, marginal leaf necrosis, stem cankers, bird's-eye fruit spots), biology, epidemiology, seed transmission, detection methods, and integrated disease management.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Speck / Tomato / Agriculture: Pest Management Guidelines / UC Statewide IPM Program (UC IPM)
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial speck appears as dark brown to black lesions of various sizes and shapes on leaves, fruit, and stems. Tissue adjacent to the lesions is initially yellow. Leaf lesions are frequently concentrated near margins, causing extensive marginal necrosis. Lesions on immature fruit are slightly raised and small, varying in size from tiny flecks to 0.125 inch (3 mm) in diameter. Caused by Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato; favored by cool, moist weather.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Speck | Cornell Vegetables
candidate_sources.snippet: Cornell Vegetables disease factsheet on Bacterial Speck of tomato caused by Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato. Covers symptoms (small dark brown to black lesions with yellow halos on leaves, raised black specks on fruit), epidemiology, and management.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Speck and Bacterial Spot in Tomatoes | Mississippi State University Extension Service
candidate_sources.snippet: Mississippi State University Extension publication describing bacterial speck (Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato) and bacterial spot of tomato — symptoms on leaves, stems, and fruit; conditions favoring disease (cool, wet weather for speck); and management recommendations including resistant cultivars, pathogen-free seed, sanitation, crop rotation, and copper-based bactericides.

candidate_sources.title: Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato (bacterial speck) | CABI Compendium
candidate_sources.snippet: CABI Compendium datasheet for Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato, the bacterial speck pathogen of tomato. Symptoms include brown-black leaf spots sometimes surrounded by chlorotic margins, dark superficial specks on green fruit, and on ripe fruit specks may become sunken and are surrounded by a zone of delayed ripening.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Speck of Tomato // Missouri Produce Growers Bulletin // Integrated Pest Management, University of Missouri
candidate_sources.snippet: University of Missouri IPM bulletin describing bacterial speck of tomato caused by Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato. Round spots develop on leaves, stems, petioles, peduncles and pedicels which are dark brown to black with a narrow yellow halo. Disease favored by leaf wetness and cool temperatures (55-77 °F).

candidate_sources.title: Vegetable: Cucumber Mosaic Virus - UMass Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Cucumber Mosaic Virus (CMV) can cause severe losses in vegetables, legumes, and ornamentals and is distributed worldwide. Has a host range of more than 1,200 species, transmitted by many species of aphids and cucumber beetles. Detailed symptoms, pathogen info, and management for tomato.

candidate_sources.title: Cucumber Mosaic Virus - Penn State Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Penn State Extension factsheet on Cucumber Mosaic Virus including symptoms (filiformity/shoestring leaves, mottling, stunting), pathogen biology (Bromoviridae family), aphid transmission, and management on tomato and other vegetables.

candidate_sources.title: Mosaic Virus Diseases Caused by Cucumoviruses / Tomato / Agriculture: Pest Management Guidelines / UC Statewide IPM Program (UC IPM)
candidate_sources.snippet: UC IPM Pest Management Guidelines for tomato mosaic virus diseases caused by cucumoviruses (CMV). Includes symptoms on tomato (thin shoestring-like leaves, yellowing, bushy stunted plants, mottled fruit), pathogen, and management recommendations.

candidate_sources.title: Cucumber Mosaic Virus of Tomato | Scouting Guides for Problems of Vegetables - University of Kentucky
candidate_sources.snippet: University of Kentucky scouting guide page dedicated to Cucumber Mosaic Virus of Tomato, covering symptoms, identification, and field scouting.

candidate_sources.title: Early Blight of Tomato | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Early blight is caused by the fungus Alternaria solani. Early blight occurs on the foliage, stem, and fruit of tomato, first appearing as small brown to black lesions on older foliage. As the lesions enlarge, they often develop concentric rings giving them a 'bull's eye' or 'target-spot' appearance.

candidate_sources.title: Early blight in tomato and potato | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Early blight in tomato and potato caused by Alternaria solani. Symptoms first appear on older foliage as small brownish to black lesions that enlarge in concentric rings. Disease develops at moderate to warm (59 to 80 F) temperatures with optimum at 82 to 86 F. Pathogen survives between crops on infected plant debris, seed, and solanaceous weeds.

candidate_sources.title: Vegetable: Solanaceous, Early Blight - UMass Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Early blight is caused by Alternaria solani and Alternaria tomatophila. Lesions develop concentric rings creating a target-spot appearance. Tissue around lesions becomes chlorotic. Fruit can become infected through the stem attachment with characteristic concentric rings. Spread by splashing water, workers, equipment and insects.

candidate_sources.title: Early Blight / Tomato / Agriculture: Pest Management Guidelines / UC Statewide IPM Program (UC IPM)
candidate_sources.snippet: UC IPM Pest Management Guidelines for Early Blight of Tomato caused by Alternaria solani. Includes symptoms, disease cycle, and management strategies including cultural practices, resistant varieties, and chemical control with fungicides scheduled by spore trapping or forecasting systems.

candidate_sources.title: Botrytis Gray Mold of Tomato | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Botrytis gray mold is caused by the ubiquitous asexual fungus Botrytis cinerea. Tomato leaf and stem lesions are tan to brown in color. Stem lesions may expand in concentric rings to girdle the entire stem causing wilting above the infection site. Fruit lesions are typically a whitish soft rot. Small 3-8 mm whitish rings on the fruit surface are aborted botrytis infections known as ghost spots.

candidate_sources.title: Gray mold of tomatoes | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Gray mold appears on young plants as gray-brown velvety mold covering stems or leaves. On fruit, green fruit decays and turns light brown or gray, starting at the point where it touches other infected plant parts. Small green fruit infected directly by airborne spores develops white, circular rings called 'ghost spots.' Infections that girdle the stem cause wilting above the infected area.

candidate_sources.title: Gray Mold / Tomato / Agriculture: Pest Management Guidelines / UC Statewide IPM Program (UC IPM)
candidate_sources.snippet: Gray Mold pest management guidelines for tomato from the UC Statewide IPM Program. Covers identification, biology, and management of Botrytis cinerea on tomato including cultural and chemical control practices.

candidate_sources.title: Gray Mold (Botrytis Blight) on Flowers, Fruits, and Vegetables (Botrytis cinerea) | Home & Garden Information Center
candidate_sources.snippet: Gray mold (Botrytis blight) on flowers, fruits, and vegetables caused by Botrytis cinerea. Clemson Cooperative Extension factsheet covering symptoms, disease cycle, and management of this widespread fungal disease.

candidate_sources.title: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum)-Gray Mold (Ghost Spot) | Pacific Northwest Pest Management Handbooks
candidate_sources.snippet: Gray Mold (Ghost Spot) of tomato caused by Botrytis cinerea. Pacific Northwest Pest Management Handbook entry on cause, symptoms, and management of this disease in tomato production.

candidate_sources.title: Late blight of tomato and potato | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Late blight is caused by the oomycete pathogen Phytophthora infestans. The first symptoms of late blight on tomato leaves are irregularly shaped, water-soaked lesions, often with a lighter halo or ring around them. Tomato fruits with late blight develop large, often sunken, golden- to chocolate-brown, firm spots with distinct rings.

candidate_sources.title: Tomato Late Blight | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Tomato late blight is caused by the oomycete pathogen Phytophthora infestans (P. infestans). On tomato leaves, late blight leads to brown-black, water-soaked, oily areas that may have a white-gray fuzzy look. The pathogen is favored by cool, wet weather.

candidate_sources.title: Late blight of potato and tomato - APS Education Center
candidate_sources.snippet: Late blight, caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans, is one of the most devastating diseases of potato and tomato. Symptoms include water-soaked lesions on leaves and firm brown rot on fruits. Management includes resistant cultivars and preventive fungicide applications.

candidate_sources.title: Late Blight | Cornell Vegetables
candidate_sources.snippet: Late blight of tomato caused by Phytophthora infestans. Leaf symptoms first appear as small, water-soaked areas that rapidly enlarge to form purple-brown, oily-appearing blotches. During high humidity, white cottony growth may be visible on the underside of the leaf.

candidate_sources.title: Phytophthora Blight : Vegetable : Center for Agriculture, Food, and the Environment (CAFE) at UMass Amherst
candidate_sources.snippet: Phytophthora Blight fact sheet covering pathogen Phytophthora capsici, host range including tomato, symptoms (crown rot, foliar blight, fruit rot with concentric rings), and management practices.

candidate_sources.title: Phytophthora blight on tomato | Long Island Vegetable Health (Cornell)
candidate_sources.snippet: Cornell Long Island Vegetable Pathology page dedicated to Phytophthora blight on tomato caused by Phytophthora capsici, including crown infections, leaf spot, foliar blight, and fruit rot symptoms; plants wilt and topple over from crown rot.

candidate_sources.title: A Diagnostic Guide for Phytophthora capsici Infecting Vegetable Crops | Plant Health Progress
candidate_sources.snippet: APS Plant Health Progress diagnostic guide for Phytophthora capsici on vegetable crops including tomato, with detailed pathogen biology, host symptoms (crown rot, foliar blight, fruit rot), and management strategies.

candidate_sources.title: PP-176/VH045: Vegetable Diseases Caused by Phytophthora capsici in Florida
candidate_sources.snippet: UF/IFAS EDIS extension publication describing vegetable diseases caused by Phytophthora capsici in Florida, including tomato Phytophthora blight symptoms (water-soaked fruit lesions with concentric rings, crown rot) and management.

candidate_sources.title: HS866/HS131: Guide to Identifying and Controlling Postharvest Tomato Diseases in Florida
candidate_sources.snippet: University of Florida IFAS Extension factsheet on identifying and controlling postharvest tomato diseases including Rhizopus rot caused by Rhizopus stolonifer, with watery lesions covered by coarse gray hairy mycelia and black sporangia.

candidate_sources.title: Rhizopus stolonifer (Soft Rot) - ScienceDirect
candidate_sources.snippet: Detailed chapter on Rhizopus stolonifer as causal agent of soft rot, a ubiquitous fungus causing postharvest soft rot on more than 200 fruit and vegetable crops including tomato. Describes water-soaked lesions, white-grey mycelium with black sporangia, and management strategies.

candidate_sources.title: Effectiveness of Chemical and Thermal Treatments on Control Rhizopus stolonifer Fruit Infection Comparing Tomato Cultivars with Different Sensitivities to Cracking
candidate_sources.snippet: Peer-reviewed article on tomato infection by Rhizopus stolonifer showing approximately 30% tomato yield loss due to this fungus; pathogen requires lesions/wounds (insect bites, splits, postharvest injuries) to initiate infection. Covers chemical and thermal control treatments.

candidate_sources.title: Rhizopus stolonifer and related control strategies in postharvest fruit: A review
candidate_sources.snippet: Review article on Rhizopus stolonifer control strategies in postharvest fruit including tomato. Disease symptoms: watery areas quickly covered by coarse gray hairy mycelia forming black sporangia. Discusses synthetic fungicides (thiabendazole, imazalil, propiconazole), biocontrol (Pantoea agglomerans, yeasts) and essential oils (Thymus, Origanum).

candidate_sources.title: Timber Rot on Tomato // Missouri Produce Growers Bulletin // Integrated Pest Management, University of Missouri
candidate_sources.snippet: Symptoms of timber rot mostly begin as water-soaked areas from the stem axils or in stem joints, either at or above the soil level. Timber rot causes loss of plant vigor, followed by wilting and eventual death of the plant. Lesions on the stem show hollow pith when cut open, and white moldy growth is usually visible at the edges of the lesions. Caused by Sclerotinia sclerotiorum.

candidate_sources.title: Frequently Asked Questions about Timber Rot on Tomato - Penn State Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Tomato timber rot, also known as sclerotinia stem rot, is a fungal disease caused by the organism known as Sclerotinia sclerotiorum. White mold is a disease of cool, wet weather – temperatures from 59 to 70°F. Timber rot of tomatoes is encouraged by prolonged periods of cool, wet conditions.

candidate_sources.title: Integrated Disease Management of Timber Rot of Tomatoes - LSU AgCenter
candidate_sources.snippet: Integrated Disease Management of Timber Rot of Tomatoes. Sclerotinia sclerotiorum can remain dormant in the soil for five years or more as hard, black sclerotia and require a conditioning period of cool temperatures before they can germinate and form apothecia — cup-shaped fruiting bodies that produce ascospores.

candidate_sources.title: White Mold / Tomato / Agriculture: Pest Management Guidelines / UC Statewide IPM Program (UC IPM)
candidate_sources.snippet: White mold generally appears on tomato plants at flowering, with water-soaked areas on flowers and at stem joints where senescent flower petals have fallen. Affected areas show white, cottony mycelium that soon produces large, irregularly shaped, black sclerotia. White mold is favored by cool (59° to 70°F), moist conditions.

candidate_sources.title: Timber Rot - Vegetable Resources - Texas A&M Aggie Horticulture
candidate_sources.snippet: Timber rot of tomato. The fungal growth progresses outward, girdling tissues and developing white, fuzzy mycelium as it grows. As the disease progresses, the plant will wilt. The tissues become even more bleached and brittle, and in seven to 10 days, the black sclerotia are formed.

candidate_sources.title: Septoria Leaf Spot of Tomato | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Septoria leaf spot is caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici. Symptoms generally develop on the lower leaves first and then the disease progresses up the plant. Septoria leaf spots appear as numerous brown spots (approximately 1/16 to 1/8 inch in diameter) on the leaves with gray or tan centers and dark brown margins. Small dark spots (pycnidia) may appear in the center of the lesion.

candidate_sources.title: Septoria Leaf Spot of Tomato
candidate_sources.snippet: Septoria leaf spot is caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici. The disease is particularly destructive in seasons of moderate temperature and abundant rainfall. The fungus is most active when temperatures range from 68 to 77 °F.

candidate_sources.title: Septoria Leaf Spot of Tomato | Plant Problems | Illinois Extension | UIUC
candidate_sources.snippet: Septoria leaf spot is a common fungal disease of tomato caused by Septoria lycopersici. Lesions also develop on stems, petioles, and calyx, although fruit are rarely affected. Symptoms develop on lower leaves first.

candidate_sources.title: Septoria lycopersici - Wikipedia
candidate_sources.snippet: Symptoms include circular or angular lesions most commonly found on the older, lower leaves, 2–5 mm in diameter with greyish center and brown margins. Often a narrow yellow halo may be observed. The lesions contain pycnidia in the center. Septoria lycopersici prefers warm, wet, and humid conditions, with optimal temperatures between 20 and 25 °C.

candidate_sources.title: Tomato Septoria Leaf Spot - University of Wisconsin Vegetable Pathology
candidate_sources.snippet: Tomato Septoria Leaf Spot caused by Septoria lycopersici. Detailed information on symptoms, disease cycle, and management for Wisconsin growers.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Wilt of Tomato | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial wilt of tomato, also known as Southern bacterial wilt, is caused by Ralstonia solanacearum (formerly Pseudomonas solanacearum). The soilborne bacterium clogs vascular tissue, causing sudden wilting of green plants, brown vascular discoloration, and white bacterial ooze from cut stems placed in water.

candidate_sources.title: Southern Bacterial Wilt of Tomato Found | University of Maryland Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Southern bacterial wilt caused by Ralstonia solanacearum has been found on tomato. Symptoms include wilted appearance of youngest leaves, wilting while still green, brown cankers at the base, vascular discoloration, and white slimy bacterial streaming from a cut stem placed in water. The disease develops in high temperatures (>85°F) and moist soils.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Wilt - Ralstonia solanacearum | Penn State Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial wilt caused by Ralstonia solanacearum (also called Southern bacterial wilt) is a destructive disease of tomato. The pathogen enters through wounds, clogs vascular tissue, and causes rapid wilting. Cultural practices and resistant cultivars are key to management.

candidate_sources.title: Southern Bacterial Wilt | National Invasive Species Information Center
candidate_sources.snippet: Southern bacterial wilt is caused by Ralstonia solanacearum. The pathogen affects tomato, potato, eggplant, and other solanaceous crops, causing vascular wilting and bacterial ooze.

candidate_sources.title: Southern Blight of Tomato and Pepper | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: On tomato and pepper, the most common symptom occurs on the lower stem where it is in contact with the soil. Initially, a brown to black lesion usually develops on the stem near the soil line. The lesion will develop rapidly and can completely girdle the stem, causing sudden and permanent wilt. Under moist conditions, white mycelium develops, with tan to reddish-brown sclerotia (1-2 mm) on the mycelial mat. High temperatures (77-95°F), aerobic and moist conditions, and acidic soil favor disease development.

candidate_sources.title: An Overview of Southern Blight, Caused by Sclerotium rolfsii | VCE Publications | Virginia Tech
candidate_sources.snippet: Southern blight is caused by the soil-borne fungus Agroathelia rolfsii (=Sclerotium rolfsii), a necrotrophic, soilborne, fungal plant pathogen present in tropical and subtropical regions worldwide. The fungus can easily infect fruit in contact with infested soil; lesions appear sunken and slightly yellow, later becoming water-soaked, soft, and star-shaped spots.

candidate_sources.title: Southern Blight – Wisconsin Horticulture
candidate_sources.snippet: A. rolfsii thrives under moist conditions. Thin existing gardens or space plants farther apart in new gardens to improve airflow and promote more rapid drying of foliage and soil. Remove all plants (including roots), as well as three inches of soil, from at least 12 inches beyond the infested area to reduce sclerotia survival.

candidate_sources.title: Athelia rolfsii (sclerotium rot) | CABI Compendium
candidate_sources.snippet: CABI datasheet covering Athelia rolfsii (synonym Sclerotium rolfsii), causal agent of sclerotium rot/southern blight, including host range, distribution, biology, symptoms, and management on tomato and other crops.

candidate_sources.title: Tomato leaf mold | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Leaf mold is caused by the fungus Passalora fulva (previously called Fulvia fulva or Cladosporium fulvum). It is not known to be pathogenic on any plant other than tomato. Discrete yellow spots on the upper side of the leaf and the corresponding olive spores on the underside are key identifying features. The oldest leaves are infected first.

candidate_sources.title: Tomato Leaf Mold | Cornell Vegetables
candidate_sources.snippet: Tomato leaf mold factsheet from Cornell Vegetables describing symptoms, pathogen biology, and management. Pale greenish-yellow spots form on the upper sides of leaves with olive-green to brown velvety mold on the lower leaf surface. Caused by Passalora fulva.

candidate_sources.title: Tomato, Leaf Mold : Vegetable : Center for Agriculture, Food, and the Environment (CAFE) at UMass Amherst
candidate_sources.snippet: Pale greenish-yellow spots, usually less than 1/4 inch, with no definite margins, form on the upper sides of leaves, and olive-green to brown velvety mold forms on the lower leaf surface below leaf spots. Tomato leaf mold is typically only an issue in greenhouse and high-tunnel tomatoes. Management includes drip irrigation, pruning for airflow, sanitation and resistant cultivars.

candidate_sources.title: Leaf Mold | Vegetable Disease Facts - Ohio State University
candidate_sources.snippet: Leaf mold caused by Passalora fulva is a common disease in greenhouses and high tunnels. High humidities above 85% are critical for spore germination. Warm temperatures of 22–24°C combined with high humidity lead to P. fulva epidemics. Resistant cultivars protect against only one to a few races.

candidate_sources.title: HPIPM: Cladosporium Leaf Mold - Bugwoodwiki
candidate_sources.snippet: Cladosporium leaf mold (Passalora fulva, formerly Cladosporium fulvum) information page covering symptoms, biology and management on tomato. Tomato is the only known host.

candidate_sources.title: Tomato Spotted Wilt Virus on Tomato and Pepper | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: TSWV is a member of the genus Orthotospovirus, a group of plant viruses vectored by arthropods, usually thrips. The pathogen infects more than 1,000 plant species among 85 families. On tomatoes, the uppermost leaves appear bronzed with small brown lesions; infected seedlings form a 'shepherd's crook' appearance and die. Fruit shows chlorotic concentric ring spots, raised bumps, uneven ripening, and deformation.

candidate_sources.title: Tomato Spotted Wilt / Tomato / Agriculture: Pest Management Guidelines / UC Statewide IPM Program (UC IPM)
candidate_sources.snippet: Pest Management Guidelines for Tomato Spotted Wilt on Tomato from UC IPM, covering identification, biology, vectoring by thrips (especially Frankliniella occidentalis - western flower thrips), and management strategies including resistant varieties with the Sw-5 gene, weed control, and thrips management.

candidate_sources.title: Tomato spotted wilt - APS Education Center
candidate_sources.snippet: APS publication providing comprehensive lesson on tomato spotted wilt virus, covering pathogen biology (Orthotospovirus, ssRNA genome), thrips transmission mechanism (acquired by larval stage, transmitted by adults), symptoms on tomato (necrotic spotting, stem necrosis, wilting, leaf curl, stunting, ringspots on fruit), and disease management.

candidate_sources.title: Tomato Spotted Wilt Virus | New Mexico State University
candidate_sources.snippet: NMSU Extension factsheet on Tomato Spotted Wilt Virus discussing symptoms, pathogen biology, thrips vectors, and management recommendations for tomato producers.
